>> Ezra Miller, aka the Bengal Ghouls, the

>> From talented indie darling to big

things like a modern-day David Bowie.
People just couldn't seem to get enough

There was just one small problem. He was

Hollywood golden boy to unemployable
pariah. So, let us learn about this
particular crash and burn together,
shall we? Ezra Matthew Miller was born
in New Jersey in 1992. One of three
children to Martr Miller, a dancer, and
Robert Miller, the president of a major
book publisher. A shy kid who suffered
from a chronic stutter, Miller enrolled
in opera classes at the age of six to
help him overcome the impediment and
ended up performing for the Metropolitan
Opera in New York. Nice stuff. But his
real passion turned out to be acting.
And at the age of 16, he landed his
first role in the low-budget drama After
School about a teenager who witnesses a
pair of girls OD while filming a school
project. The movie was a modest success
in film circles and got just enough
recognition to win more roles. And the
following year, he was acting opposite
Andy Garcia in the dramdy City Islands.
But it was the chilling 2011
psychological drama We Need to Talk
About Kevin that really put him on the
map. Surprise, surprise. Miller plays a
troubled teenager who has a
dysfunctional relationship with his
mother that eventually escalates into a
full-scale massacre at his local high
school. It's not exactly a fun watch,
but credit where credit's due, Miller is
superb in it and more than capable of
holding his own against a veteran actor
like Tilda Swintson. By now, his star
was very much on the rise, and Miller
was gaining a reputation as the exciting
new talent in town, able to play complex
and challenging psychological roles, but
just as comfortable doing breezy
romantic dramas like Madame Bovery and
The Perks of Being a Wallflower.
Hollywood was taking notice of Ezra
Miller, and it wasn't long before bigger
roles started coming his way. The first
of these was the announcement of his
casting as the Flash in Zack Snider's
doomed DC universe. In retrospect, it
was probably a bad move, but at the
time, it seemed like a huge opportunity.
And whatever you might think about the
movies he was actually in, Miller seemed
to understand the assignment when it
came to playing Barry Allen. He brought
just the right amount of nervous energy
and neuroticism to the character,
balancing out the stodgy seriousness of
the rest of the cast. Unfortunately,
Justice League, his first big outing as
the character, turned out to be a
troubled production. Snider was forced
to bow out halfway through the process,
and Jos Weeden's hasty course
corrections didn't do much to help the
movie, which got roasted by critics and
disappointed at the box office. Still,
Miller's momentum didn't seem to be
impacted too badly, and his recurring
role in the Fantastic Beasts franchise
turned out to be just the saving grace
he needed to stay on top. Ezra Miller
was still very much in demand, a
talented actor whose attention-grabbing
outfits, daring photooots, and weirdly

played into the image of a vibrant and
complex artist just bursting to express

when Miller's parents announced their

He became isolated, withdrawn, and
paranoid, he started wearing a
bulletproof vest and carrying a gun
wherever he went. Convinced that the KKK
were out to get him for some reason.

This was a man unraveling in real time
in front of the entire world. And it all
happened at the worst possible time for
Warner Brothers. They were already
fighting a losing battle to save the
troubled Flash movie, which was going
through major re-shoots that had blown
the budget up to almost $350 million.
Having the star of what they were trying
to market as a familyfriendly superhero
film get exposed as a psychopath, a
predator, and a religious cult leader
was basically the final nail in the
coffin for the Flash. For a time, there
were even serious talks of cutting their
losses and cancelling the film
altogether. Ultimately though, the best
they could do was push back the film's
release, limit Miller's social media
presence, and quietly shuffle him away
from the limelight, desperately hoping
that people would somehow forget all the
controversies and accusations against
him. As it turned out, they didn't. The
film bombed big time, and with it went
the best chance of saving the DCU and
the last hopes of salvaging Miller's
Hollywood career. All the controversies
and meltdowns of 2022 didn't just derail
his momentum, they [ __ ] killed it.
Stone deads. In the three years since
the Flash debacle, Ezra Miller hasn't
appeared in a single movie or TV show of
any kind, and there are no real plans
for any upcoming projects. Hollywood
might be a haven for weirdos and
oddballs, and willing to forgive all
manner of transgressions, but there are
levels of controversy that even they
can't sweep under the rug. For all
intents and purposes, Miller is persona
non-grata in the industry now. too
radioactive to touch, too unreliable to
hire, and without the box office capital
and industry clout to get people to care
about him again. And it's a shame really
because there's a reality where Miller
was able to forge a successful career as
a challenging and unconventional artist,
breaking barriers and challenging norms,
but it's not this one. Ultimately, for
all his talent and potential, Ezra
Miller was undone by his own demons and
personal hang-ups. He's a sobering
reminder that fame isn't for the
fragile, that success comes at a cost,
and that even Hollywood can only
tolerate so much bad behavior from its
stars.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
